result:
I’d give every person,…a built-in, one-time, irresistible pause-button that springs open at the precise instant they’re about to do anything that would irreversibly hurt another living being. The pause lasts only a heartbeat, but inside it there is perfect silence and absolute empathy—not an afterthought, not a regret, but an instant transfusion of what the other will feel.

Nothing else changed in human nature except the impossibility of forgetting that single moment of shared sensation. No need for war rooms, ceasefires, walled borders, or weapon embargos; the torment one is poised to unleash becomes as vivid as one’s own impending pain. A tyrant signing an execution order gasps with the condemned. A paramilitary officer about to level a village feels the dust in a grandmother’s lungs.

The moment would belong to each person alone—the state can’t confiscate it, propaganda can’t drown it. It isn’t imposed conscience; it’s irrefutable data, wired into the nervous system. The choice to stop or continue would still be theirs, but collusion in atrocity would now require a kind of superhuman cruelty—the deliberate violation of a shared self. I suspect that, once, would be enough.

History’s weight of un-taken pauses—at executions recorded and battles scrawled in ruins—would not magically vanish, but the day after the change the ledger would begin to stand still, each future stroke lighter until recruiting atrocity itself becomes unthinkable, like sawing off our own legs.

That’s the single alteration: one impossible heartbeat of perfect empathy, seeded in every body forever.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
